title: Major Trauma — Primary Survey
oneLiner: Systematic ABCD assessment for life-threatening injuries in the first gold hour — do not leave the resuscitation room before completing it.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Absent breath sounds + hyper-resonance + tracheal deviation + hypotension ± distended neck veins. Immediate decompression (needle thoracostomy or finger thoracostomy) — not a chest X-ray.
- **Massive haemorrhage (internal/external):** Ongoing bleeding with refractory hypotension despite 2L blood; pelvis open-book or femur fracture; FAST positive. Apply pelvic binder, direct pressure, call for massive transfusion protocol immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible trauma (RTC, fall, stabbing) or mechanism suggesting high energy
  - Reduced conscious level (GCS < 15)
  - Breathlessness, hypotension, tachycardia
  - Obvious external bleeding or deformed limb
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Airway – hoarse voice, stridor, gurgling, blood/saliva in pharynx  
  - Breathing – absent/reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation, chest wall instability, open wounds  
  - Circulation – pale, cold, weak/absent peripheral pulses, capillary refill > 2 s, distended neck veins (tamponade/tension)  
  - Disability – GCS, pupil size and reaction, limb movement  
  - Exposure – log roll to examine back, perineum, axillae; ensure warm environment
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Haemorrhagic shock (internal/external)** – flat neck veins, responsive to blood (not crystalloid)
- **Tension pneumothorax** – distended neck veins but unilateral absent breath sounds + hyper-resonance
- **Cardiac tamponade** – distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds + hypotension (Beck’s triad)
- **Spinal shock** – flaccid areflexia, no motor response, bradycardia (not tachycardia)
- **Obstructed airway (foreign body/aspiration)** – sudden onset, no chest injury, unilateral breath sounds + rhonchi
- **Splitter:** Distended neck veins with hypotension → tension pneumothorax (unilateral breath sounds) vs tamponade (bilateral normal breath sounds + muffled heart sounds) → eFAST confirms pericardial fluid.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Primary survey sequence – do not pause for X-ray unless clinical indication  
  - **eFAST** (extended Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) – look for intra-abdominal free fluid, pericardial effusion, pneumothorax  
  - **Chest X-ray** – only after decompression if still unstable; check for haemothorax, pneumothorax, widened mediastinum  
  - **Pelvic X-ray** – if haemodynamically unstable or pelvic fracture suspected  
  - **ABG** – lactate, base deficit, pH, pCO₂, K⁺  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting profile, crossmatch (type & screen or group O in emergency), pregnancy test (all females 12–55)  
  - Lactate, toxicology screen if altered consciousness  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** Whole-body CT (pan-scan) in stable or stabilised patient with blunt trauma and high-risk mechanism (NICE NG39)  
  - **Second-line:** Targeted CT (head, chest, abdomen) if single region suspected and haemodynamically stable  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **CT angiography** – if suspected aortic injury (widened mediastinum on CXR)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**  
  - **ISS (Injury Severity Score)** – used for audit & prognosis; clinical use is limited  
  - **MT (Massive Transfusion) prediction** – ABC score (shock + penetrating + FAST + arrival base > 6 mM): ≥ 2 points suggests need for massive transfusion  
- **Never-miss test:** **eFAST** – omission can delay detection of pericardial tamponade or intra-abdominal haemorrhage, leading to preventable death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **A** – jaw thrust (not head tilt if c-spine at risk), suction, adjuncts (OPA/NPA), if obstructed → call airway expert (anaesthetist/ITU) for RSI; consider surgical airway if cannot intubate/ventilate
  - **B** – high-flow O₂ (15 L non-rebreather); decompress tension pneumothorax (needle 2nd ICS MCL then chest drain in 5th ICS AAL); if open pneumothorax, cover with occlusive dressing taped on three sides
  - **C** – IV/IO access (two large-bore cannulae), warm blood (O-negative women, O-positive men) in shock; apply pelvic binder if pelvis unstable; direct pressure on external bleeding; TXA 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g over 8 h; start massive transfusion protocol if ongoing haemorrhage (target Hb > 80, platelets > 50, fibrinogen > 1.5)
  - **D** – GCS, pupils; if GCS ≤ 8 → intubate; consider ketamine for sedation/pain
  - **E** – log roll, check back/flank/perineum/axillae; prevent hypothermia (forced-air warmer, warm fluids); remove clothing
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - ICU admission for all major trauma patients  
  - Ongoing resuscitation: goal-directed (lactate clearance, base deficit correction)  
  - Definitive surgery (laparotomy, thoracotomy, fixation, vascular repair) from 2nd hour onward  
  - Reassess and repeat primary survey after every intervention  
  - Secondary survey (head-to-toe, including PR, urine output, neuro exam)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Rehabilitation (physiotherapy, occupational therapy)  
  - Psychological support (PTSD screening)  
  - Fracture clinic, pain clinic as needed  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** Warmed blood (1–2 L, then guided by response) or balanced crystalloid (Plasmalyte) if no blood available; avoid large-volume crystalloid (dilutional coagulopathy)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not started until haemorrhage controlled and no active bleeding; then LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) after 24–48 h if no contraindication  
  - **Diet/NBM:** Nil by mouth until airway secured or decision made (may need CT with IV contrast)  
  - **Observations:** q15 min in first hour, then hourly if stable; continuous monitoring (HR, BPs, SpO₂, capnography if intubated)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Tranexamic acid (TXA): 1 g IV over 10 min stat, then 1 g over 8 h (CRASH-2 protocol)  
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IVPRN **[check local policy for head injury]**  
  - **RSI drugs:** Ketamine 1–2 mg/kg IV + rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV **[check local policy]**  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Fentanyl 1–2 µg/kg IV for haemodynamically unstable  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ketamine – avoid if raised ICP (relative)  
  - TXA – caution if anaphylaxis history; no major drug interactions  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - TXA – no adjustment; rocuronium – prolonged effect in hepatic failure  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **RSI paralytics** – giving a depolarising agent (suxamethonium) if unknown hyperkalaemia risk (crush injury, burns) → cardiac arrest; **always** check K⁺ or use rocuronium if unsure
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Missed injury (e.g., hollow viscus perforation, diaphragmatic rupture)  
  - Abdominal compartment syndrome (tense abdomen, oliguria, raised airway pressures)  
  - Coagulopathy, hypothermia, acidosis (lethal triad)  
  - Acute kidney injury, rhabdomyolysis  
  - Ventilator-associated pneumonia  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Post‑traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)  
  - Chronic pain, neuropathic pain  
  - Heterotopic ossification, contractures  
  - Venous thromboembolism
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality in major trauma (ISS ≥ 15) ~ 10–20%; highest in first 1–2 h from haemorrhage or TBI. Timely primary survey and damage‑control surgery improve survival. Recovery depends on injury pattern; many survivors have long‑term disability.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always repeat the primary survey** after every intervention (intubation, chest drain, fluid bolus).  
- **Apply a pelvic binder early** for any patient with pelvic fracture or persistent shock – it reduces venous bleeding.  
- **Get a trauma CT as soon as haemodynamically stabilised** – don’t waste time on repeated plain films.  
- **Use eFAST liberally** – it changes management (tamponade → thoracotomy; free fluid → laparotomy).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay chest decompression for tension pneumothorax** to wait for X‑ray.  
- **Do not give large volumes of crystalloid** – worsen clotting and hypothermia; give blood.  
- **Do not forget to expose the back** (log roll) – 30% of injuries are missed posteriorly.  
- **Do not clear the cervical spine** until imaging and clinical exam are complete.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- (Not applicable for admitted trauma patients – minor trauma only)  
- **Return if:** worsening pain, shortness of breath, vomiting, seizure, loss of consciousness, or new neurological symptoms.  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 72 h; trauma clinic if fracture or soft‑tissue injury; head injury advice leaflet provided.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call trauma team leader / senior anaesthetist:**  
  - Airway obstruction despite adjuncts  
  - Failed intubation / cannot ventilate  
  - Hypotension unresponsive to 2 L blood  
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L after initial resuscitation  
  - GCS drop ≥ 2 or pupillary change  
- **Call ICU / crash team:**  
  - Cardiac arrest (traumatic – reversible causes: tension, tamponade, hypovolaemia, hypoxia)  
  - NEWS2 > 7 (but in trauma, use haemodynamic and respiratory parameters)  
  - Ongoing haemorrhage requiring massive transfusion (> 10 U PRBCs in 6 h)

rawMarkdown:
## Major Trauma — Primary Survey
**One-liner:** Systematic ABCD assessment for life-threatening injuries in the first gold hour — do not leave the resuscitation room before completing it.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Absent breath sounds + hyper-resonance + tracheal deviation + hypotension ± distended neck veins. Immediate decompression (needle thoracostomy or finger thoracostomy) — not a chest X-ray.
- **Massive haemorrhage (internal/external):** Ongoing bleeding with refractory hypotension despite 2L blood; pelvis open-book or femur fracture; FAST positive. Apply pelvic binder, direct pressure, call for massive transfusion protocol immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Visible trauma (RTC, fall, stabbing) or mechanism suggesting high energy
  - Reduced conscious level (GCS < 15)
  - Breathlessness, hypotension, tachycardia
  - Obvious external bleeding or deformed limb
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Airway – hoarse voice, stridor, gurgling, blood/saliva in pharynx  
  - Breathing – absent/reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation, chest wall instability, open wounds  
  - Circulation – pale, cold, weak/absent peripheral pulses, capillary refill > 2 s, distended neck veins (tamponade/tension)  
  - Disability – GCS, pupil size and reaction, limb movement  
  - Exposure – log roll to examine back, perineum, axillae; ensure warm environment

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Haemorrhagic shock (internal/external)** – flat neck veins, responsive to blood (not crystalloid)
- **Tension pneumothorax** – distended neck veins but unilateral absent breath sounds + hyper-resonance
- **Cardiac tamponade** – distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds + hypotension (Beck’s triad)
- **Spinal shock** – flaccid areflexia, no motor response, bradycardia (not tachycardia)
- **Obstructed airway (foreign body/aspiration)** – sudden onset, no chest injury, unilateral breath sounds + rhonchi
- **Splitter:** Distended neck veins with hypotension → tension pneumothorax (unilateral breath sounds) vs tamponade (bilateral normal breath sounds + muffled heart sounds) → eFAST confirms pericardial fluid.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Primary survey sequence – do not pause for X-ray unless clinical indication  
  - **eFAST** (extended Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) – look for intra-abdominal free fluid, pericardial effusion, pneumothorax  
  - **Chest X-ray** – only after decompression if still unstable; check for haemothorax, pneumothorax, widened mediastinum  
  - **Pelvic X-ray** – if haemodynamically unstable or pelvic fracture suspected  
  - **ABG** – lactate, base deficit, pH, pCO₂, K⁺  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting profile, crossmatch (type & screen or group O in emergency), pregnancy test (all females 12–55)  
  - Lactate, toxicology screen if altered consciousness  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** Whole-body CT (pan-scan) in stable or stabilised patient with blunt trauma and high-risk mechanism (NICE NG39)  
  - **Second-line:** Targeted CT (head, chest, abdomen) if single region suspected and haemodynamically stable  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **CT angiography** – if suspected aortic injury (widened mediastinum on CXR)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**  
  - **ISS (Injury Severity Score)** – used for audit & prognosis; clinical use is limited  
  - **MT (Massive Transfusion) prediction** – ABC score (shock + penetrating + FAST + arrival base > 6 mM): ≥ 2 points suggests need for massive transfusion  
- **Never-miss test:** **eFAST** – omission can delay detection of pericardial tamponade or intra-abdominal haemorrhage, leading to preventable death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **A** – jaw thrust (not head tilt if c-spine at risk), suction, adjuncts (OPA/NPA), if obstructed → call airway expert (anaesthetist/ITU) for RSI; consider surgical airway if cannot intubate/ventilate
  - **B** – high-flow O₂ (15 L non-rebreather); decompress tension pneumothorax (needle 2nd ICS MCL then chest drain in 5th ICS AAL); if open pneumothorax, cover with occlusive dressing taped on three sides
  - **C** – IV/IO access (two large-bore cannulae), warm blood (O-negative women, O-positive men) in shock; apply pelvic binder if pelvis unstable; direct pressure on external bleeding; TXA 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g over 8 h; start massive transfusion protocol if ongoing haemorrhage (target Hb > 80, platelets > 50, fibrinogen > 1.5)
  - **D** – GCS, pupils; if GCS ≤ 8 → intubate; consider ketamine for sedation/pain
  - **E** – log roll, check back/flank/perineum/axillae; prevent hypothermia (forced-air warmer, warm fluids); remove clothing
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - ICU admission for all major trauma patients  
  - Ongoing resuscitation: goal-directed (lactate clearance, base deficit correction)  
  - Definitive surgery (laparotomy, thoracotomy, fixation, vascular repair) from 2nd hour onward  
  - Reassess and repeat primary survey after every intervention  
  - Secondary survey (head-to-toe, including PR, urine output, neuro exam)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Rehabilitation (physiotherapy, occupational therapy)  
  - Psychological support (PTSD screening)  
  - Fracture clinic, pain clinic as needed  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** Warmed blood (1–2 L, then guided by response) or balanced crystalloid (Plasmalyte) if no blood available; avoid large-volume crystalloid (dilutional coagulopathy)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not started until haemorrhage controlled and no active bleeding; then LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) after 24–48 h if no contraindication  
  - **Diet/NBM:** Nil by mouth until airway secured or decision made (may need CT with IV contrast)  
  - **Observations:** q15 min in first hour, then hourly if stable; continuous monitoring (HR, BPs, SpO₂, capnography if intubated)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Tranexamic acid (TXA): 1 g IV over 10 min stat, then 1 g over 8 h (CRASH-2 protocol)  
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IVPRN **[check local policy for head injury]**  
  - **RSI drugs:** Ketamine 1–2 mg/kg IV + rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV **[check local policy]**  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Fentanyl 1–2 µg/kg IV for haemodynamically unstable  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ketamine – avoid if raised ICP (relative)  
  - TXA – caution if anaphylaxis history; no major drug interactions  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - TXA – no adjustment; rocuronium – prolonged effect in hepatic failure  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **RSI paralytics** – giving a depolarising agent (suxamethonium) if unknown hyperkalaemia risk (crush injury, burns) → cardiac arrest; **always** check K⁺ or use rocuronium if unsure

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Missed injury (e.g., hollow viscus perforation, diaphragmatic rupture)  
  - Abdominal compartment syndrome (tense abdomen, oliguria, raised airway pressures)  
  - Coagulopathy, hypothermia, acidosis (lethal triad)  
  - Acute kidney injury, rhabdomyolysis  
  - Ventilator-associated pneumonia  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Post‑traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)  
  - Chronic pain, neuropathic pain  
  - Heterotopic ossification, contractures  
  - Venous thromboembolism

### Prognosis
- Mortality in major trauma (ISS ≥ 15) ~ 10–20%; highest in first 1–2 h from haemorrhage or TBI. Timely primary survey and damage‑control surgery improve survival. Recovery depends on injury pattern; many survivors have long‑term disability.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always repeat the primary survey** after every intervention (intubation, chest drain, fluid bolus).  
- **Apply a pelvic binder early** for any patient with pelvic fracture or persistent shock – it reduces venous bleeding.  
- **Get a trauma CT as soon as haemodynamically stabilised** – don’t waste time on repeated plain films.  
- **Use eFAST liberally** – it changes management (tamponade → thoracotomy; free fluid → laparotomy).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay chest decompression for tension pneumothorax** to wait for X‑ray.  
- **Do not give large volumes of crystalloid** – worsen clotting and hypothermia; give blood.  
- **Do not forget to expose the back** (log roll) – 30% of injuries are missed posteriorly.  
- **Do not clear the cervical spine** until imaging and clinical exam are complete.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- (Not applicable for admitted trauma patients – minor trauma only)  
- **Return if:** worsening pain, shortness of breath, vomiting, seizure, loss of consciousness, or new neurological symptoms.  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 72 h; trauma clinic if fracture or soft‑tissue injury; head injury advice leaflet provided.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call trauma team leader / senior anaesthetist:**  
  - Airway obstruction despite adjuncts  
  - Failed intubation / cannot ventilate  
  - Hypotension unresponsive to 2 L blood  
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L after initial resuscitation  
  - GCS drop ≥ 2 or pupillary change  
- **Call ICU / crash team:**  
  - Cardiac arrest (traumatic – reversible causes: tension, tamponade, hypovolaemia, hypoxia)  
  - NEWS2 > 7 (but in trauma, use haemodynamic and respiratory parameters)  
  - Ongoing haemorrhage requiring massive transfusion (> 10 U PRBCs in 6 h)
